title: Anatomy of a Data Breach
intro: It's 2 AM and your phone is blowing up. A user just tweeted a screenshot showing they can see other users' private data on your app. Just like a burst pipe, your first job is to stop the leak before doing anything else. This is a real security incident, and every decision you make in the next few minutes matters.

teach.title: A Data Breach Is Like a Burst Pipe
teach.body: When a pipe bursts in your house, the first thing you do is shut off the water -- not mop the floor, not call the plumber, not post on social media. In a data breach, your first move is the same: stop the leak before doing anything else.

teach.title: Incident Response Is Like an ER Triage
teach.body: When someone arrives at the emergency room, doctors don't start with paperwork -- they stabilize the patient first, then diagnose, then treat. A data breach works the same way: contain the bleeding (stop the leak), assess the damage (check logs), notify the family (tell affected users), and then perform surgery (fix the root cause). Skipping straight to the fix while data is still leaking is like scheduling surgery while the patient is still in the ambulance.

teach.title: The Five Phases of Incident Response
teach.body:
When a security breach happens, walk through these phases in order:

1. **Detect** -- recognize that an incident is happening and confirm it's real
2. **Contain** -- stop the leak immediately (disable the endpoint, revoke the key)
3. **Eradicate** -- remove the vulnerability and any attacker access
4. **Recover** -- restore service safely and notify affected users
5. **Postmortem** -- learn from it and put guardrails in place so it can't happen again

teach.title: A Common API Vulnerability
teach.annotation: The vulnerable version lets anyone fetch any user's data just by changing the ID in the URL. The fix adds authentication and checks that you can only access your own data.

teach.body: If you're building apps with AI and selling them to clients, a single data breach can destroy the trust you worked so hard to build -- and kill your revenue overnight. Knowing how to respond to incidents isn't just a skill, it's insurance for your business.

Detect, contain, eradicate, recover, learn. Every API endpoint needs authorization checks -- never assume the frontend will prevent unauthorized access -- and every incident should leave behind a guardrail that prevents recurrence.

start.narrative: DETECT. You see the tweet: a user navigated to /api/users/42 and got back another user's full profile -- email, phone, hashed password. Before you do anything else, you need to confirm this is real. What's your first move?

choices.text: Reproduce the request yourself against staging to confirm the leak is real
Reply to the tweet thanking them for the report
Check if other endpoints might have the same issue first

reply-tweet.narrative: Public acknowledgment is fine later, but you haven't even confirmed the bug is real -- and the endpoint is still leaking while you compose tweets. Confirm first, contain second, communicate last.

choices.text: Right -- reproduce against staging first to confirm

check-others-first.narrative: Scope mapping is smart, but the reported endpoint is actively leaking right now. An attacker could be scraping every profile while you wander other code. Confirm and contain the known leak first, then widen the search.

choices.text: Reproduce the reported endpoint, then move to containment

contain.narrative: CONTAIN. The bug reproduces in staging. The endpoint is genuinely returning any user by ID. You need to stop the bleeding right now. What's the fastest, safest way to plug it?

choices.text: Add auth middleware that returns 403 unless req.user.id matches the requested id, deploy hotfix
Take the entire /api/users route offline at the load balancer
Just block the IP from the tweet so they can't keep scraping

eradicate.narrative: ERADICATE. The reported endpoint is locked down. But the bug pattern -- assuming the frontend would never request someone else's id -- might exist elsewhere. How do you find and remove the rest?

choices.text: Grep every route handler for unauthenticated :id params and add the same auth check
Add a global authorization middleware that defaults to deny and opt routes in explicitly
Wait and see if anyone reports another vulnerable endpoint

recover.narrative: RECOVER. Logs show 347 sequential ID requests from one IP over 6 hours -- up to 347 users had data scraped. Service is stable again. What does responsible recovery look like?

choices.text: Email affected users with what was exposed, force password reset, and notify your DPO/legal
Quietly fix it -- public notification will spook customers
Notify only the most active 50 users and skip the rest

postmortem.narrative: POSTMORTEM. Service is recovered, users are notified. The last job is making sure this class of bug can never ship again. What goes into the writeup?

choices.text: Document timeline, root cause, write authorization tests, add a CI lint that flags routes without auth, schedule quarterly auth audits
Write a short Slack message: 'fixed the BOLA bug, won't happen again'
Skip the postmortem -- the team is exhausted and the fix is in

end-good.narrative: You followed the full incident response loop: confirmed the leak, contained it with a targeted hotfix, eradicated the pattern across the codebase, recovered with honest user notification and legal compliance, and turned the incident into permanent guardrails -- tests, lint rules, and a recurring audit. The next contributor cannot accidentally ship the same bug.
end-good.feedback: Textbook incident response. The vulnerability you handled is called Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA), the #1 issue on the OWASP API Security Top 10. The reason it stays #1 is that most teams stop at containment. You went all the way to prevention -- that's how breaches stop repeating.

end-mediocre.narrative: The fire is out, but no document, no automated check, no scheduled review. In six months a new contributor adds /api/orders/:id without auth, and you're back here.
end-mediocre.feedback: You handled the live incident competently. Skipping the postmortem is where the cost compounds: incidents that don't generate guardrails are doomed to recur. A 30-minute writeup and a CI lint cost less than another breach.

end-poor.narrative: No notification, no postmortem, the team moves on. Months later another endpoint is exploited the same way. By then, the original incident is lost institutional memory -- and possibly a regulatory violation, since you skipped notification.
end-poor.feedback: Each phase you skipped multiplies the cost. Notification is often legally required (GDPR, CCPA, breach notification laws). A postmortem is what converts a bad night into permanent learning. Without either, the breach repeats and the legal exposure grows.
